retrieved_docs.title: Bladder Cancer -- Evaluation -- High-Risk Patients
retrieved_docs.content: Patients considered high risk or who fail to qualify as low or intermediate risk should be evaluated with a computed tomography (CT) urogram (ie, CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis without and with IV contrast) and a cystoscopy.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Radiation Oncology -- Complications from External Beam Radiation Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: There is an increased risk of hematuria in up to 15% of patients, especially if anticoagulated. Managing hemorrhagic complications of radiation cystitis includes oral pentosan polysulfate and hyperbaric oxygen therapy. Severe hematuria may require cystoscopy and continuous bladder irrigation. If unsuccessful, bladder instillations with 1% alum, aminocaproic acid (Amicar), and formalin may be required (up to a 10% formalin solution can be used, but 4% is the preferred concentration). [301]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostate Cancer -- Radiation Oncology -- Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: The role of stereotactic radiotherapy in prostate cancer is less well-defined compared to standard external beam radiation. With stereotactic therapy, the individual fractionated dosages are higher, typically 7 to 8 Gy each, which allows for a much reduced total treatment time, typically only about 1 week. Higher fractionated dosages beyond 8 Gy are not recommended as they have been associated with increased toxicity and adverse effects. Stereotactic radiotherapy is less suitable for patients with large prostate volumes (greater than 75 to 100 mL) or prior TURP surgery. Most experts prefer real-time tracking, and early reports suggest that using urethral catheterization during treatment planning and simulation improves urethral identification. Newer stereotactic ablative radiotherapy (SABR) delivery systems include gantry devices currently undergoing clinical trials. Using SABR for metastatic cancer may be reasonable to reduce the seeding of additional tumors, which may ultimately increase overall and progression-free survival (PFS). This strategy has already been shown to improve survival in metastatic non–small cell lung cancer but is still theoretical for use in prostate cancer. [294] [305] [306]

retrieved_docs.title: Brachytherapy -- Clinical Significance -- Prostate Brachytherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Prostate brachytherapy features prominently in the treatment of prostate cancer. It may be used as monotherapy in low and favorable intermediate-risk prostate cancer or as a boost when combined with external beam radiotherapy in high-risk disease. In addition, some centers have used brachytherapy for salvage therapy in recurrent cases. LDR and HDR modalities have both been utilized with excellent outcomes. Contraindications to prostate brachytherapy include distant metastasis, unacceptable operative risk, large prostate urethral defects, ataxia telangiectasia, absence of rectum, and limited life expectancy. [4] Relative contraindications include prostate gland size greater than 60 ccs, IPSS scores greater than 20, inflammatory bowel disease, and large median prostate lobe.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Treatment of Delayed Radiation Injury -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: The effects of ionizing radiation on soft tissues are permanent and ever-changing. Patients treated with radiation therapy for prostate cancer can develop post radiation cystitis and hematuria even 20 years after the completion of the radiation therapy. Patients treated for prostate and colon cancers can develop proctitis as well as cystitis due to late effects of ionizing radiation. [12] [13] [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.content: Most bladder cancer cases are low-grade and superficial, with minimal health risks if treated promptly and monitored appropriately according to guidelines.

retrieved_docs.title: Localized Prostate Cancer -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: When considering treatment, large randomized controlled trials have demonstrated equivalent overall and disease-specific mortality of active surveillance, radical radiotherapy, and radical surgery at 10 years of follow-up. Thus, it is important to assess each patient individually to establish his co-morbidities and priorities to optimize treatment and minimize side effects (for example, not offering radiotherapy to a patient with pre-existing colitis).

The American College of Radiology recommends a total dose of 75 to 78 Gy. The radiation oncologists use a total dose of 77.4 Gy at the institution. retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Cystitis and Hyperbaric Management -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation cystitis describes the inflammation and resultant cellular destruction of the bladder that occurs as an adverse effect of the radiation used in the treatment of cancers. Typically, radiation cystitis occurs after radiation of the pelvic region, which may be required for treatment of primary bladder cancer or treatment of tumors in regions around the bladder, colon, rectum, ovaries, uterus, and prostate. Damage from radiation can be acute, occurring less than six months from completion of radiation therapy, or delayed, occurring more than six months after completion of radiation therapy. Severity of functional impairment and irritation vary. Milder symptoms may include increased frequency, urgency, and dysuria and may resolve with time. Severe symptoms may include urinary incontinence, gross hematuria, and progression of damage to include fistula formation or necrotic bladder tissue. This activity reviews the pathophysiology, evaluation, and management of radiation cystitis and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for patients affected by this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Bladder Cancer -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Screen for symptoms and risk factors associated with bladder cancer in high-risk populations. Assess the TNM and clinical staging used in the classification and evaluation of bladder cancer. Apply the latest therapeutic guidelines in the treatment of both non-muscle-invasive and muscle-invasive bladder cancer. Communicate the importance of collaboration among an interprofessional team to enhance the delivery of care for patients affected by bladder cancer. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 719
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions constitute a broad clinical category that encompasses specific bladder disorder (اضطراب في المثانة). This category frequently co‑occurs with urinary stone disease (حصاة حالبية) and with benign prostatic hyperplasia (فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد), and it is clinically associated with prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا). Transurethral resection of prostate (TURP) is a surgical intervention that is clinically linked to benign prostatic hyperplasia (فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد) as well as to general prostate enlargement (تضخم بروستاتي). TURP is also associated with prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا), reflecting its role in the diagnosis and management of malignant prostate disease. The interconnections among genitourinary symptoms, bladder disorder, urinary stones, benign and malignant prostate conditions, and the TURP procedure underscore the importance of integrated assessment and treatment strategies in urologic patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1808
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions arise within the urinary system and frequently co‑occur with prostatic disorders, seminal calculi, and both localized and metastatic prostate cancer; they are also clinically associated with prostate cancer. The prostate is an organ that belongs to the urinary system and serves as the anatomic site for prostate‑related diseases, including metastatic prostate cancer. Prostatic disorders are situated in the prostate, and other male genital disorders are likewise located in the prostate, representing subcategories of prostatic disorders. These other male genital disorders are linked to screening activities. Together, these relationships emphasize that disturbances of the prostate and associated genitourinary symptoms are central to the presentation, co‑occurrence, and clinical management of prostate disease, particularly cancer, and underscore the importance of appropriate diagnostic screening.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2865
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions are frequently observed together with حصاة حالبية, indicating that patients presenting with vague or nonspecific genitourinary complaints may also have ureteral stones. In addition, these genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions are clinically associated with سرطان البروستاتا, suggesting that prostate cancer can manifest with or be accompanied by such nonspecific genitourinary findings. The same category of genitourinary symptoms and ill-defined conditions also co‑occurs with سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي, reflecting that metastatic prostate cancer may present with similar ambiguous genitourinary manifestations. Recognizing these overlapping relationships is important for clinicians to consider a broad differential diagnosis, ensure appropriate imaging and laboratory evaluation, and promptly identify underlying urolithiasis or prostate malignancy, including its metastatic form.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 623
retrieved_docs.content: Transrectal biopsy of prostate is classified within عمليات البروستاتا, the broader category of prostate procedures. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: Transurethral resection of prostate (TURP) is clinically associated with فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد (benign prostatic hyperplasia), تضخم بروستاتي (prostatic enlargement), and سرطان البروستاتا (prostate cancer). TURP is commonly performed to relieve urinary obstruction caused by benign prostatic hyperplasia and other forms of prostatic enlargement, providing symptomatic improvement and facilitating bladder emptying. In the context of prostate cancer, TURP may be used to obtain tissue for histopathological diagnosis or to palliate obstructive symptoms in advanced disease. By addressing both benign and malignant prostatic conditions, TURP plays a pivotal role in the therapeutic and diagnostic management of patients with prostate-related disorders.

These procedures are also related to other male genital disorders classified under prostatic disorder (اضطراب بروستاتي) and linked to preventive screening (التحصّي). Collectively, the interrelated classifications, anatomical sites, disease entities, and procedural links provide a comprehensive framework for understanding the spectrum of genitourinary malignancies, associated bladder and prostate disorders, and their diagnostic and therapeutic interventions in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1834

This network of relationships indicates that patients presenting with genitourinary symptoms should be evaluated for possible urolithiasis, benign prostatic hyperplasia, and both localized and metastatic prostate cancer, and that TURP is a common therapeutic intervention for managing prostatic enlargement and prostate cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 1027
retrieved_docs.content: Herniated urinary bladder and genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions are both classified as disorders of the bladder (اضطراب في المثانة). The category of genitourinary symptoms and ill‑defined conditions frequently co‑occurs with حصاة حالبية and with فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد, and it is clinically associated with prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا). Transurethral resection of prostate (TURP) is clinically linked to the same benign prostatic condition (فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد), to prostatic enlargement (تضخم بروستاتي), and also to prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا). These relationships highlight the close connection between bladder disorders, stone disease, benign prostatic hyperplasia, prostatic enlargement, and prostate cancer, as well as the therapeutic relevance of TURP in managing benign and malignant prostatic pathology.
